title: Internal Certificate Authority (PKI)
description: Deploy a self-managed internal Certificate Authority for issuing X.509 certificates to services, enabling mTLS, client authentication, and zero-trust networking. Covers common open-source CA implementations: HashiCorp Vault PKI (recommended for dynamic short-lived certs), Smallstep CA/step-ca (ACME-compatible, developer-friendly), cfssl (Cloudflare's PKI toolkit), and OpenSSL-based manual CAs. Works in air-gapped and on-premises environments where external CAs (e.g., Let's Encrypt) are inaccessible.

cost.costNotes: All major CA implementations (Vault, step-ca, cfssl, OpenSSL) are open-source at no license cost. Infrastructure cost: small VM or container (~$10-50/month) to run CA service. Vault OSS is free; Vault Enterprise adds HCP Vault pricing. Step-ca and cfssl are free. Air-gapped/on-prem deployments have no SaaS costs.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: CA server requires minimal compute: a t3.micro (~$8/month) or equivalent is sufficient for most internal PKI workloads. Min=0 for on-prem bare-metal or existing infrastructure; max=50 covers a dedicated small cloud VM with storage.
provenance.adoptionCost: PKI setup has non-trivial complexity: designing root/intermediate CA hierarchy (1-2 hours), deploying CA server (1-2 hours), integrating certificate issuance with services (2-4 hours), establishing rotation procedures (1-2 hours). Total: ~8 hours at $50/hour = $400. AI tools help with Vault policy authoring and certificate config.
provenance.licenseCost: HashiCorp Vault OSS, Smallstep step-ca, cfssl, and OpenSSL are all open-source with no license fees. HashiCorp changed Vault to BSL in 2023; OpenBao is the OSS fork if BSL is a concern.

provides.reasoning: This pattern is specifically designed to establish and operate a Certificate Authority that issues X.509 certificates to services and clients.
Internal CA handles the full certificate lifecycle: issuance, renewal, revocation (CRL/OCSP), and rotation.
Deploying an internal CA establishes a complete Public Key Infrastructure with root/intermediate CA hierarchy.
Internal CA issues the client and server certificates required to establish mTLS connections between services.
Short-lived certificates issued by internal CA are a foundation for zero-trust service identity (e.g., SPIFFE/SPIRE integration).

requires.reasoning: CA server requires a host (VM, container, or bare-metal) to run on. For air-gapped environments this is physical infrastructure.
CA state (root key, certificate database, CRL) requires durable persistent storage.
CA server should be network-isolated — only certificate issuance endpoints exposed, root CA key kept offline or in HSM.

availability.reason: All major CA implementations are open-source and publicly available

supports_nfr.reason: Internal CA is only needed when mTLS is the authentication mechanism. OAuth2/OIDC, JWT, and API key auth do not require X.509 certificates issued by an internal CA.
Internal CA availability depends on deployment architecture; single-node CA is typically 99.9%, HA cluster can reach 99.99%.
CA recovery requires restoring encrypted key material and state from backup; minimum RTO is 5 minutes for warm standby.
With synchronous replication or HSM-backed storage, CA can achieve near-zero RPO for certificate issuance state.
Internal CA can meet HIPAA requirements for encryption key management and certificate-based authentication when properly configured with audit logging.
Internal CA is GDPR-neutral; certificate Subject fields may contain personal data requiring appropriate handling.
Internal CA is CCPA-neutral; certificate management has no direct consumer data implications.
Internal CA with audit logging supports SOX access control and change management requirements.

supports_constraints.reason: Internal CA can be deployed on any cloud (Vault on EC2/AKS/GKE, step-ca on any container platform) or on-premises. Essential for air-gapped on-prem environments where external CAs are inaccessible.
X.509 certificate integration is available in all languages via TLS libraries (OpenSSL, BoringSSL, native TLS stacks). CA interaction uses standard ACME, EST, or HTTP APIs.
Internal CA issues certificates for services across all platforms; particularly critical for API and backend service mTLS.

ca_implementation.description: CA software. vault: HashiCorp Vault PKI secrets engine — best for dynamic short-lived certs and Kubernetes integration. step-ca: Smallstep — ACME-compatible, developer-friendly, good for zero-trust. cfssl: Cloudflare PKI toolkit — lightweight, REST API. openssl: manual OpenSSL CA — maximum control, highest operational burden.

ca_hierarchy.description: CA hierarchy depth. root-only: simpler but root key exposed for issuance (not recommended for production). root-intermediate: root CA kept offline; intermediate CA issues certs — standard production practice for protecting root key.

key_algorithm.description: Key algorithm for CA and issued certificates. ecdsa-p256 recommended: smaller keys, faster TLS handshakes, strong security. rsa-2048/4096 for legacy compatibility with older clients or compliance requirements specifying RSA.

cert_validity_days.description: Default certificate validity period in days. Short-lived certs (1-90 days) reduce revocation burden and limit blast radius of compromised certs. Longer validity (1-3 years) reduces rotation operational overhead. 90 days aligns with Let's Encrypt precedent.

enable_ocsp.description: Enable OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) for real-time certificate revocation checking. Recommended for high-security environments. Requires CA server to be reachable by all certificate consumers; not suitable for fully air-gapped environments.

warn_nfr.reason: Single-node CA is typically 99.9%; targets above 99.9% require HA CA cluster with standby replicas.
warn_nfr.message: pki-internal-ca: availability target {actual} requires HA CA cluster deployment. Single-node CA is ~99.9%. Deploy active-standby (step-ca HA, Vault HA with Raft) to meet higher targets.
